ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for boiled lamb with broth; closest_parent 'lamb food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents cooked sheep/lamb meat; no specific code for boiled lamb with broth dish exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227197003 represents lamb as a substance; no specific concept for boiled lamb with broth dish; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of boiled lamb with broth is approximately 350 g, comprising bone-in lamb pieces and accompanying soup broth.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 in fat, choice, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17003); adapted for boiled preparation using USDA boiled lamb shoulder data (NDB 17010) and broth dilution factor
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy boiled/cooked lamb entries (NDB 17010: Lamb, domestic, shoulder, arm, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/4 in fat, choice, cooked, braised) and adjusted for the inclusion of broth (~30% broth by weight in the as-served dish). Per-100g values reflect the composite dish including broth, resulting in diluted macros relative to pure cooked lamb. Sodium is elevated due to salted broth preparation typical of Xinjiang-style hand-grabbed lamb.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat dishes with negligible carbohydrate content have an effectively zero glycemic index as they contain no significant glycemic carbohydrates. Per University of Sydney GI Database convention and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008), meat-only dishes are not assigned a GI value. glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value, effectively zero) to reflect the absence of significant glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is 'low' by convention; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g per serving is negligible (0.3 g per 100g × 350g serving = ~1.05g carbohydrate, GL < 1).

hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving of boiled lamb with broth provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt broth preparation.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled lamb with broth contains approximately 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g and has a negligible glycemic load, making it a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium intake from the broth should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled lamb provides approximately 2.1 g saturated fat per 100 g and elevated sodium from the broth; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume moderate portions and prefer leaner cuts with reduced-sodium broth to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled Lamb with Broth (手抓肉带汤汁, Shǒu zhuā ròu dài tāng zhī) is a traditional Xinjiang and Inner Mongolian Chinese dish consisting of bone-in lamb pieces slow-boiled in lightly seasoned water with aromatics such as onion, ginger, and black pepper, then served with the resulting broth. The name 'hand-grabbed meat' (手抓肉) refers to the traditional practice of eating the tender lamb pieces by hand. The dish is a staple of Xinjiang Muslim cuisine and is commonly served at Northwest Chinese restaurants. The lamb is minimally seasoned to preserve the natural flavour of the meat, resulting in a clean, savoury broth and tender, mildly flavoured lamb. The texture is soft and yielding due to prolonged boiling. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~5.4 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (~0.3 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. It is a good source of zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and niacin. The sodium content is elevated due to the salted broth, which is a clinical consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. In Singapore, it is served at Xinjiang and Northwest Chinese restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific dish (boiled lamb with broth, Xinjiang style) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy boiled lamb entries with a broth dilution adjustment. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input error) to 'Meat and alternatives' as this is a lamb dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for boiled lamb. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for boiled lamb. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 for pure boiled lamb with broth; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — lamb contains no dietary fibre. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 and vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked lamb with no vegetable additions. 6. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) reflecting the absence of significant glycemic carbohydrates in this meat-and-broth dish; gi_category is 'low' by convention; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as the carbohydrate content per serving is negligible. This aligns with the rule that glycemic_index must be greater than zero. 7. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g is an estimate for the as-served dish including salted broth; pure boiled unsalted lamb would have lower sodium (~65 mg per 100 g per USDA). 8. The GI source note documents that pure meat dishes are not assigned GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database; the 1 value and 'low' category reflect this convention. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g values adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 17010.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives'.
Added canonical English food_name 'Boiled Lamb with Broth'.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional local names.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirement.
Populated full nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted for boiled lamb with broth.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) with explicit notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae) with confirmed zero justification.
Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1 to comply with the 'greater than zero' rule for negligible carbohydrate foods, updating associated notes and source explanations.
Added defensible ontology anchors with closest_parent match_type and gap notes.
Populated health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance.
Ensured aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
